title: Understanding the Problem
text: To find out how much the climber stretches the rope, we need to use the formula for the elongation of a material under stress. This involves the Young's modulus, the force applied, the original length of the rope, and the cross-sectional area of the rope.

title: Formula for Elongation
text: The formula for elongation (ΔL) is given by: $$ΔL=\frac{F L}{A Y}$$, where F is the force applied, L is the original length, A is the cross-sectional area, and Y is the Young's modulus.

title: Calculate the Force
text: The force (F) exerted by the climber is equal to her weight, which is mass times gravity. Calculate F using $$F=m g$$, where $$m=65.0$$ kg and $$g=9.8$$ m/s².

title: Cross-sectional Area of the Rope
text: The cross-sectional area (A) of the rope can be calculated using the formula for the area of a circle: $$A=\pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2$$, where $$d$$ is the diameter of the rope.

title: Calculate the Force
text: Calculate the force exerted by the climber using her mass and gravity.

title: Calculate the Cross-sectional Area
text: Calculate the cross-sectional area of the rope using its diameter.

title: Calculate the Elongation
text: Use the formula $$ΔL=\frac{F L}{A Y}$$ to find the elongation of the rope.
